feedback.text: The main issue I want to get across right now is with regards to payment processor censorship. Paypal, Visa, Mastercard and Stripe have forced storefronts such as Steam and Itch.io to remove adult content, or content they deem as adult, even ignoring the PEGI age rating systems. This has negatively affected adult creators and LGBTQ+ works, as Conservative organisations that have lobbied for this, such as Collective Shout, view LGBT+ peoples' existence as pornographic. Payment processors should not be allowed to police what people spend their money on, or what companies should be allowed to sell. We're seeing the results as companies like YouTube and Discord roll out age verification measures that don't work. Discord has banned people for being under 18 due to their appearances when they're significantly older than that. This is a severe violation of privacy and safety, and does nothing to protect kids either. Another issue is how many apps are forcing people into allowing their posts and works on social media to be used to train AI algorithms. Again, this is a major breach of privacy and safety as people's data can be used by AI algorithms for nefarious purposes such as propaganda or unconsented harmful content of these individuals, or even just stealing artwork, resulting in artists and creatives losing their jobs. Speaking of which, video game preservation is a severe issue. As games continue to go all-digital, publishers such as Ubisoft have been taking down failed projects that people have paid for and they can no longer access them. The petition for Stop Killing Games reached well over 1.4 million signatures so hopefully this is a factor that's being considered. There needs to be more measures to protect individuals from the overreach of companies that are invading people's privacy by age verification systems that don't work and give way more information than they should be allowed to have, protect from censorship by payment processors as they shouldn't be in charge of what people can and can't buy. The digital world is increasingly unsafe, and people who claim to be advocates of digital safety want nothing more than to police the internet. The internet needs to be safer, yet freer. I hope that the Digital Fairness Act will aid in all of these key issues.
